narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57.4% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 1 and 2 respectively, and family income in the lower-middle bracket, among other details like your full-time enrollment plans. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best signals bar exam success. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 decile ranking, which placed you at the lower end compared to the typical top-decile performance of students who passed the bar. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile, also on the lower side relative to passers, further nudging the model toward concern about your trajectory. Positively offsetting this was your undergraduate GPA, which stands above the average of 3.24 for those who passed, helping pull the prediction somewhat toward success. While your LSAT score of 32 is below the 37.27 average for bar passers, and your lower-middle family income is less common among them (where middle and upper-middle brackets dominate), these played supporting roles rather than leading ones. Overall, your profile shows strengths like that solid undergrad performance, but the early and later school deciles raised the biggest red flags when compared to accepted students, who typically rank much higher in those metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starts have improved dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, seeking ways to strengthen your class rankings through tutoring or study groups, and perhaps gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view in your favor, as even small improvements in key areas make a big difference for students like you.
